title: Reduced-State Stabilizer Rényi Entropy as a Probe of Quantum Criticality in the Transverse ANNNI Model and the Quantum Compass Model
authors: Santanu Sarkar, George Biswas, Jun-Yi Wu, Anindya Biswas
abstract: We investigate the effectiveness of the stabilizer Rényi entropy (SRE), a quantifier associated with non-stabilizer resources (quantum magic), as an indicator of quantum phase transitions. Specifically, we analyze the behavior of the purity-corrected SRE of reduced density matrices in the ground states of two one-dimensional spin models: the transverse axial next-nearest-neighbor Ising (TANNNI) model and the quantum compass model (QCM). The ground state of the TANNNI model is obtained using exact diagonalization techniques, while the QCM is analyzed using the Jordan--Wigner (JW) transformation followed by Bogoliubov diagonalization of the resulting quadratic fermionic Hamiltonian. For the TANNNI model, the purity-corrected SRE successfully detects the antiphase--floating phase transition in the high-frustration regime, while in the low-frustration regime the raw (purity-uncorrected) SRE reproduces the known ferromagnetic--paramagnetic phase boundaries more accurately. For the QCM, the purity-corrected SRE exhibits a clear signature near the isotropic point \(J_x/J_z=1\), where the system undergoes a first-order quantum phase transition. Our results establish SRE of reduced states as a complementary probe of quantum criticality and provide further insight into the role of non-stabilizer resources in many-body quantum phase transitions.
summary: This paper evaluates the effectiveness of using Stabilizer Renyi Entropy as a local probe for quantum criticality. It demonstrates that while purity-corrected SRE is excellent for detecting certain transitions in frustrated models, the raw SRE may be more reliable in low-frustration regimes. The study provides a new way to use quantum magic as a signature of phase transitions.
main_problem: Investigating whether the Stabilizer Renyi Entropy (SRE) and its purity-corrected version can serve as effective local probes for detecting quantum phase transitions.
main_result: The purity-corrected SRE successfully detects the antiphase-floating transition in the high-frustration TANNNI model and the first-order transition in the Quantum Compass Model, while the raw SRE is more accurate for the low-frustration TANNNI regime.
method: The authors use Exact Diagonalization for the TANNNI model and Jordan-Wigner/Bogoliubov transformations for the QCM, applying finite-size scaling to the derivatives of the SRE.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the 1D Transverse Axial Next-Nearest-Neighbor Ising (TANNNI) model and the 1D Quantum Compass Model (QCM).
key_observables: Second-order Stabilizer Renyi Entropy (SRE), purity-corrected SRE, and their first derivatives with respect to control parameters.
important_parameters: Transverse field strength (gamma), frustration ratio (J2/J1), and anisotropy ratio (Jx/Jz).
main_assumptions: The purity-corrected SRE is used to mitigate the effects of mixedness in reduced density matrices, though it is noted not to be a true magic monotone for mixed states.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the TANNNI phase diagram; Figures 2-6 display SRE and its derivatives for various TANNNI regimes; Figure 7 compares extracted transition lines to established boundaries; Figure 13 shows SRE for the QCM.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the SRE as a probe, describes the TANNNI and QCM models, details the analytical and numerical methods (JW transformation, BdG, ED), presents results for high and low frustration regimes in TANNNI, analyzes the QCM transition, and concludes with a comparison of SRE types.
why_interesting: It explores how non-stabilizer resources (quantum magic) can act as a complementary diagnostic tool to traditional entanglement entropy for identifying phase transitions in frustrated many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
